title: Who Will Pay for Tariffs? Businesses' Expectations about Costs and Prices
summary: A Boston Fed survey of 500-600 small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) conducted December 2024 to August 2025 finds that SMBs significantly raised their tariff expectations from roughly 19% to 25% on average during the first four months of 2025. Actual tariff rates paid by affected importers nearly doubled from 6.5% in January 2025 to 11.4% in July 2025. The authors estimate a 0.75% near-term increase in core consumer prices from tariff costs on directly imported goods, with importers indicating they would pass through about two-thirds of anticipated cost increases into prices within one year. SMBs expecting tariffs to persist over a year planned to pass through up to three times more cost increases than those expecting short-lived tariffs.
key_points: SMBs' average expected tariff rate rose from ~19% to ~25% between early 2025 and August 2025
Actual average tariff rate paid by affected importers nearly doubled from 6.5% (January 2025) to 11.4% (July 2025)
Estimated 0.75% near-term increase in core consumer prices attributable to higher tariff costs on directly imported consumer goods
SMBs expecting tariffs to persist >1 year anticipate passing through up to 3x more cost increases than those expecting short-lived tariffs
As of August 2025, >45% of SMBs affected by new tariffs expected their costs to be impacted for longer than one year
Importer pass-through rate jumped to ~2/3 by April 2025, up from baseline ~1/3 observed through February 2025
why_it_matters: This research provides direct survey evidence on how businesses intend to transmit tariff costs to consumer prices, offering a forward-looking channel for tariff pass-through into inflation. The finding that over 45% of affected SMBs expect tariff impacts to persist beyond one year suggests businesses are pricing in sustained trade policy changes rather than temporary disruptions. This has implications for the path of core inflation and Federal Reserve policy considerations, though the authors explicitly note these are their own views and not official Federal Reserve positions.
macro_context: The survey provides empirical evidence for the inflation transmission mechanism of tariffs. The estimated 0.75% near-term increase in core CPI from direct tariff pass-through represents a meaningful but contained inflation impulse from this specific channel. The distinction between short-duration vs. persistent tariff expectations driving a 3x difference in planned pass-through rates suggests that perceived tariff duration is a critical determinant of business pricing behavior. The passthrough rate jump from ~1/3 baseline to ~2/3 for importers following the April 2025 reciprocal tariff announcement indicates businesses adjust pricing strategies as policy uncertainty resolves.

issues_found: Source body was truncated, ending mid-sentence at 'pass-through rate for no' — the August 2025 non-importer pass-through conclusion is missing from the provided text. The record correctly captures importer pass-through but this gap limits full verification.

Publication age (2025-09-29) is approximately 6 months before TODAY (2026-04-01), exceeding the 90-day threshold. The record appropriately notes this but the source still has archival value given its empirical findings on tariff pass-through mechanisms.

human_review.notes: The record is well-constructed and accurately summarizes the source's key findings. Minor uncertainty stems from source truncation (missing non-importer August conclusion) and publication age, but these do not materially undermine the record's accuracy. Auto-routing to review_queue is appropriate.

notes: This is a high-quality research commentary from the Boston Fed analyzing SMB tariff expectations and pricing behavior. The source is directly relevant to macro catalysts through its analysis of tariff pass-through channels into consumer prices. The survey methodology (Morning Consult, nationally representative SMB sample, multiple waves) provides a robust basis for findings. Confidence slightly lowered due to publication age exceeding 90-day threshold. Source explicitly disclaims institutional endorsement. Truncation at end of source body leaves uncertainty about final August 2025 pass-through conclusions for non-importers.

reasoning: Empirical research with primary survey data from a nationally representative SMB sample
Clear methodology with multiple survey waves allowing temporal comparison
Authors are affiliated with Boston Fed with relevant expertise in macroeconomics
Provides specific quantitative estimates (0.75% CPI impact, pass-through rates) with explicit caveats
Explicitly notes limitations and that views are authors' own, not institutional
